mnemonics: FILES-DICT: Files In Linux Environment Store Data - Directories In Core Tree
UNIX-DATA: User Needs Indexed eXchange - Dictionaries Arrange Text Access
keyPoints: Files in UNIX are treated as a sequence of bytes, allowing flexible data handling without imposed structure.
System calls like open(), read(), write(), and close() are used for low-level file operations.
File descriptors are small integers used to reference open files within a process.
Dictionaries (or associative arrays) are not native in C but can be implemented using structures and hash tables for key-value storage.
The Standard I/O library provides buffered streams (FILE *) for more efficient file handling compared to system calls.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to check the return value of file operations, leading to unhandled errors.
Not properly closing files after operations, which can cause resource leaks.
Confusing file descriptors (low-level) with file pointers (streams in stdio).
Assuming dictionary keys are stored in order, whereas hash tables do not guarantee ordering.
Mismanaging memory in dictionary implementations, especially when inserting or deleting key-value pairs.
